speaker: interviewer
text: Take number four, interview with Mr. David Shear. It is 12, 21st, 1994. Eh, will you please tell me, how did you get from east to the west?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got through transportation from the-- from the British. They-- they provided. So we came to the first city in the west, which it was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And over there, we just start to organize ourselves. There were a lot of people who came-- who made this trip from the east. They escaped. They didn't want to be with the Russians, so they-- with communism. So they came to the west. And with the help of the military government, we went into-- we took over one of the hotels, which belonged to one of the big Nazis, big German Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Golden Korona, The Golden Crown. The Golden Korona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Goslar exactly? Can you describe where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goslar was in a very beautiful-- between the hills and [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means it's not Germany. It is right next to Braunschweig, if you know where Braunschweig is, or not far from Hanover. But Hausberg, this is a-- it's a very beautiful center-- you know, part of Germany. And we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I want you to tell me about your activities in Goslar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So what we did is we took over-- we went to one of these hotels. We were told that the owner was one of the big, uh, leaders of the Nazi party, of Hitler's gang. Anyway, and they gave us this hotel to establish that all the people who came from-- from camps should live there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we organized ourselves, and we took one office, one room for an office. And we divided our type of work. I became, uh, in charge of, uh, the educational part of the youngsters, which they were-- they didn't know anything about Judaism or about anything humane. And everybody was ready to kill everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, so we organized. And, of course, the UNRRA-- UNRRA was the organizations which they-- it was in Bergen-Belsen, the central-- the central station. So we had to go in to register that we have so many people to feed, and they gave us food to organize a kitchen. And, uh, I'm making it short, because there is so much that happened, but we just want to give you the gist of this. We organized, and everybody got a beautiful room. It-- it was the same service as the hotel used to have before, except under new management. And, um, we had to organize entertainment and speeches and tell the people about-- about all their responsibility, new responsibility as human being. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many youngsters were there at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all together about 300 people, which-- in this-- in this hotel, which there were at least 170, 200 young people, mostly. And, of course, over there we started to live like normal people. They-- they had at time Russian, um, rationing, which they get. And they were a part of that. And food, we gave them free, and lodging. And we thought they should have whatever their needs were to-- we used to get it from the UNRRA, which it was the-- from the United States. And we start to live a normal life as much possible. And, of course, this was in 1945, and by year or so later, we started to organized youth, [NON-ENGLISH], to go to Israel, especially before '47, when the-- with the independence of Israel. We tried to, uh, see that a lot of youngsters were able to go to Israel and to be a part of this. And in my case-- by the way, and meanwhile, I met sister while I was in Goslar. My sister showed up. She tried to find me while she came back from Siberia. She came back to Germany, one of these base camps. And she had a-- a family, beautiful, nice family together. So I was able to send my sister to Israel with her family. Of course, myself, I wanted to go to Israel because I spoke Hebrew, and I was-- the only life that I was hoping to be in Israel. But circumstances didn't want me to go to Israel at the time, which probably I couldn't say it was a mistake, because thank God it wasn't. Because I came to the States, and I was lucky to-- to put my life together-- not the way I wanted, but somehow I made a go of it. I became a manufacturer. I couldn't put my knowledge or my experience to use because I didn't speak the language, and I was too lazy or too much--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you come to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to the States in 1950-- in 1950. Because in 1948 when we were finished, we sent the people to Israel and the rest supposed to have been themselves. And then some of them decided to stay in Germany for a while. But I couldn't go to Israel, so-- Of course, somehow when my sister returned from-- from Russia and she went to Israel, I realized that she would, from now on, depend on me as a leader of her family, as a member of the surviving family. And I sort of didn't have the feeling of, uh-- to take over responsibility of her-- of her family and all. And I didn't have the feelings for-- family feelings. I knew I had a sister, a half sister, but [INAUDIBLE]. Now, of course, she had children, and I didn't. So I said, I can always go to Israel from America. But thank God it worked out that I came to the States. And I couldn't go back to teaching. I couldn't go back to everything that I was hoping to. So I became a man of business. I became a-- a manufacturer, a millinery manufacturer. But this is a long story how I got to hat factory, to hat manufacturing. But somehow, and I made it. And, uh, then I met a lovely, uh, girl, also by one of those things in life that you never plan. And-- and all of a sudden we, two years later, we were surprised that we are with a daughter. And this was the biggest thing in my life, which I never thought it. I can't help it. I must go back. When Jacob saw Joseph and he said, [HEBREW], I never thought I'm going to see your face, [HEBREW], and now I see your children, he said. This is a wonderful thing that I could never, never believe it could happen. And thank God, my daughter, she developed to be a nice, young lady. I was able to give her the wonderful schooling, which I was-- to me, it was most-- more than anything else in the world. She graduated from a-- she went to Dartmouth College. She went to, um-- Yeshiva here to this Sephardic-- I became a Sephardi, by the way, from Polish old [INAUDIBLE], because my wife is a Sephardic. She's from the island of Rhodes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I joined a Sephardic community here in-- in New York, which my daughter took part in this-- in their education. So she got a beautiful, nice Jewish education. She reads Hebrew well. She understands Hebrew. She's observant as much as most of the Jewish families are.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was able to graduate from Harvard, which she had a secondary-- she went for an MBA from Harvard University after she graduated from Dartmouth. Uh, and thank God she has a wonderful husband. Her husband has a PhD, and he is a, uh-- also in the computer business. She is doing quite well. She is marketing for American Express, for IDS. And I have two beautiful grandchildren. One is Joshua-- Joshua [? Parrish, ?] which he is three years old. And Reuben, God bless him, he is 17 months old. My daughter's name is Gabrielle and David Shear. And, uh, well, sometimes you count your blessings, as they say. And the rest-- the rest of the-- of the-- my history is for the world to judge. If this world could--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message or lesson do you have for your children and future generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was going to say it, and that's what the purpose, I think, for my conversation would be there is-- King Solomon says somewheres, [HEBREW]. A human being was born to be like a wild mule without any consideration of anything. And this mule, this wild mule, has to be tamed. Our type of Judaism that we have, of not being-- not to do this and not to do that, this is a part of Jewish religion. To be a Jew is to do things, not to be passive-- to be active, to be responsible as a Jew, to support Jewish life, and to talk about Judaism. And Judaism is not just things you do or you don't. Judaism is a-- a way of living, to educate your children and how, what is up to the individual quality. Of course, one thing, you've-- people have to be responsible for human being, for each other, and especially for your family, not just to let it go and to say, well, because I was good or I did well, my children will. No, you have got to be after, and you've got to build a Jewish home. You've got to be able-- this is the humane thing to do. And things like this should never happen again. This is for the world to decide. No matter how much we try, if it is Israel or it is the rest of the part of the world, wherever we turn around, there is misery. And I suppose that's what they meant of the days of the messiah. Because one day, the human race will realize they must solve their own problems, and they must be able to learn how to live to each other and to be responsible for each other. Well, I hope it will serve some good purpose. Thank you for-- for this interview. And I hope it will do any good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Shear and Mrs. Shear, you were married in 1957.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, right here. Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York? Eh, you have mentioned in the interview that you became a Sephardi. Would you please, eh, explain how you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my wife through a blind date. At work-- I didn't know-- somebody went to a bat mitzvah. And he said, did I meet a beautiful, lovely girl. She is the-- the life of the party.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, well, I-- you know, as I said in my interview-- never thought to continue life with much, uh, glamor or much anticipation. So I said, listen, I could use any friend, especially-- especially a female friend. So-- and we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, at the time, she was working as a-- at the Art Student League. She was quite a-- quite a young lady. She is still today. But at the time, I was a little more easy with her because I never wanted to meet anybody who has too much attachments with family. We talked-- I had no family, and I never wanted a big crowd around me. She fit at that moment just perfect, because she had no family. She was alone here. I knew she had some sisters, she told me, but far away. So-- and so we started to date, and the rest is history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Shear, would you tell me, please, when you met this guy, how did you-- how did you find him? He was old. He must have been heartbroken.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. He was in pretty good shape. And he was very jolly, very, uh, you know, outgoing. And that's how I went out with him. Because, you know, I was used to-- you know, to a lot of, uh-- you know, at the Art Students League, I met so many people, and it had to be-- you know, it had to be somebody that, uh-- that would interest me and that would fit what I needed to have around, you know. And that's what had happened. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got married, I understand that he became part of your life, part of the Spanish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, because first of all, we got married at the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue. And then, um, he was-- at the beginning, he was going to a Ashkenazic synagogue, and I was going to my Sephardic synagogue because I couldn't-- you know, I wasn't used to it. But then when our daughter was born, and then she started going to Hebrew school, then, uh-- so he decided. He says, listen, we have to go one place, because we cannot be split. So we-- he decided to come to-- and I converted him to Sephardic. But he still is very much involved with the other synagogues, too. I mean, once in a while he sneaks out, and he goes to his synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak the same language?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak Polish or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Esther doesn't speak a word of-- now she knows a few word of Yiddish, a few word of Hebrew, but not too much, because she took a few lessons. And, uh, her speciality-- we are between us, we do quite well. I speak the Eastern languages. I speak German quite well and, of course, Hebrew. And a little bit of Russian I could say to myself. And all the-- But she is a, uh, word encyclopedia herself. She went to French schools. She, uh, speaks Spanish, which it is a-- I still have a problem whenever I-- I listen conversationally. It is-- it's mostly Spanish and French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born, Esther?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the island of Rhodes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your Spanish upbringing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I didn't have much of a Spanish upbringing because when I was born, my parents decided to move to Paris. So we-- we left Rhodes when I was about a year and a half. And I was brought in Paris until I was about 11 years old. And then when my father decided to retire, we went back to Rhodes. And then over there, I had to learn again, because Rhodes belonged to Italy at the time. So I had to learn Italian, go to school in Italy. And that's how it happened that I learned my, uh-- my Italian, and then my French, of course. It was like my mother tongue, actually, because I was born with it. And, uh-- and then we-- I decided when we had the racial laws in Italy, I couldn't go to school anymore. So my father decided, well, you have to get out of here because I want you to go to school. And at the time, I had a sister living in Paris, and I had a sister who was teaching at a university in Turin, in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, uh, I decided that I would go first to Italy to stay with my sister for a while. And maybe I would go to France after that. But somehow in between all these things-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] You were in Turin you were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Turin. And what happened is after all these things, my sister received a letter from the American consulate from Naples, that she had an affidavit from my uncles who were living in Seattle, Washington. And so she went for the, uh, interview. And, of course, the consulate was very impressed because at the time, you know, that's 50 years ago, more than that, and she spoke fluent English. And they became quite friendly. And so she told me, she says, you know, I can't go because I have a little sister who is here with me. So he says, well, we'll try to see if the affidavit is good enough for both of you, which it was. So instead of going to France, I came to the United States with her. That's how-- that's how it happened that I came to the United States. And never-- I never thought that I wasn't going to see my parents anymore. I mean, I thought I was going to go there for just a while, and that was it. That's how--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your profession? Were you an artist? You went to, uh, an art school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not an artist, but I worked in an art school. I went there to work in the-- as an office-- you know in the office. I was a registrar and, you know, took care of many-- interviewed students and tell them the curriculum, what to take, what not to, advise them in their courses. And, um, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you raise your daughter, with a Polish influence or Sephardic influence? With Sephardic cookies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sephardic cookies, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The goods, Sephardic cookies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sephardic-- Sephardic influence more or less, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter actually took her from her mother, most of-- well, most of it, a lot from me. I think she has my stubbornness, my-- some of my philosophy in life she has. I was able to catch her off guard, you know? But-- because she always used to say, oh, here comes your sermon. But when I used to, uh, pick her up and take her someplace, drive someplace, but I used to talk like talking to myself. She knew at the time talking to her. But, uh, she, uh-- so she got her mother's system, she-- By the way, Mrs. Shear is a very, exceptionally good cook. And, uh, and she learned-- she learned from her mother. She is also a very-- she likes to invent, you know, all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you a question. Did your daughter ever ask you about your experiences in the war? Did she ever want to know? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my daughter-- my daughter is after me for the last number of years that, uh, I should record my life history, or my experiences. But somehow, you know, there's certain things about the past. You're better off when you talk to somebody. When you talk to yourself, somehow there's so many things you want to put on tape, and, uh, unless you-- you're a writer or you-- you concentrate, but otherwise I think I would be able to put on tape so much, if I talk to somebody, which it-- somehow you make sense. You-- you express yourself. And so my daughter knows a lot about the past. As a matter of fact, when she went to Europe, she went to visit, uh, places in Europe. Yeah, she's much more aware of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Poland, to Ostrowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in a million years. I wouldn't go on the Polish-- I wouldn't step on Polish ground for no money in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been to Germany since?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but it's amazing. In Germany is where I started my life all over again. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And, uh-- as a-- as a people, I'm not trying to reform the world, but-- or to change the-- the opinion of the world. But I would rather-- if I had a choice and I want to go to visit Germany. I wouldn't feel as bad as I would felt to go and visit Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever talk to you about his experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, many times. Many, many times, of course-- of course. And I-- I tell you, he says he would like to go to Germany. I wouldn't go to Germany. I don't think I would like to go to Germany at all. I mean, knowing that what happened to my parents, I-- that's one thing that I-- it's a no-no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the reason that you-- in [? Slavia ?] you did not have a bad experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's not-- exactly. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: The five years he was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but, well, I am thinking about what happened before. I mean, I wasn't there, so I-- I cannot compare, and I cannot, uh, give my opinions as far-- but as far as I'm concerned, I've never been to Germany, and I wouldn't go. I mean, I have no desire to go back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you resent Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't say that I resent them for the things they did. I resent that-- But, uh, as the-- the-- to be-- for the country itself, I wouldn't mind, let's say, visit and having a good time and, uh, to forget-- I wouldn't look at every German that he is the one who killed, uh, some of the Jews or something. Because I met through the years, while I was dealing with them, some mostly sincere people, people who are in the educational field, which they were sincere about the mistakes that, uh, they made about being negligence not to know, to be led by-- by whatever had happened. But I don't justify it, God forbid-- not for one moment. But I had very-- my life rebuilded in Germany, and I got a lot of, uh. Help. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Maybe it was their guilt, which it helped, that they did-- they felt guilty and they tried to, uh, go along. So-- but I wouldn't-- it wouldn't be my favorite place to go there to have a good time, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell your grandchildren about the camps, about Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're too little. They're only three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have little children. I have small ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when they'll grow up, would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's an important document.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sure that, uh, their mother and their father will tell them. And, uh, my daughter is very much aware of, uh-- my past. She's much aware of, uh, what my life, my priorities in life are. So I'm sure that she'll never miss-- she'll have a chance to explain to them how everything came-- originated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Shear, there is anything that you want to tell me that I did not ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Thank you for asking.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am very interested about the gefilte fish and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --excellent, uh, Sephardic cookies, how it goes together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually I-- I do mostly Sephardic cooking, but I learned gefilte fish because, you know after all, I have to please my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she improved it quite a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I improve it, and people, you know, like it. And, uh-- but I usually-- I have a variety of cooking. I mean, I do French, I do Chinese, I do a little bit of everything. So it's not really-- there's no influence of, uh, you know-- of course, the, uh, Sephardic influence is-- I mean, is prevalent, is the first thing. But otherwise, it's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been to Israel or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, a few times. Yes, we have been.

speaker: interviewee
text: What a question. Israel we have been to many times. The only thing, the last few times, the last few summers my health wasn't up to it, so-- but, uh, Israel is one of my favorite place where I feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: He likes to go to Israel a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: --feel at home wherever I am. Whatever-- we used to say, [HEBREW], the air is full of knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have friends from 'Lumold' still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: Luboml.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't. I don't. Somehow the friends from-- the people who are in Luboml, they are from a different generation. They are a different quality of people, because they are-- when I left as a youngster, 12, the people of Luboml, which I left behind, were of a different world. They are-- association with life was simple survival. In Yiddish, the way we know-- but the people that I-- I befriended with later, they were more my educational field, more in my-- so-- and also more friends that I accumulated through my wife-- she has some Sephardic, some very nice people. We became very friendly. And, of course, Sephardim-- let's face it-- are more sophisticated than the Russian or Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE]. And, uh, I sort of-- on account of her, I sort of-- I didn't change my life, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: But nevertheless, he's always criticizing the Sephardim. He-- he likes the Ashkenazim. Don't let him tell you anything else. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he does.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just give credit where credit is due. And, uh--
